one_line_takeaway: Robust object detection is mostly a backbone problem: initialize with an adversarially pre-trained backbone and cheaply adversarially fine-tune (FreeAT + AdamW + 0.1x backbone LR), and detectors beat prior AT defenses -- which a stronger PGD evaluation shows were a false sense of security.
key_finding: Adversarially pre-trained backbones, long neglected by prior detector-AT work, are the dominant factor for robust detection. On PASCAL VOC (eps=8), prior defenses collapse under the stronger attack -- AARD was claimed at 41.5% AP50 under Acls but scored worse than a standard model (~0.7 AP50) once the PGD step size was tightened. The recipe lifts robustness substantially: e.g., Faster R-CNN VANAT rises from 19.0 to 32.2 AP50 under Acls; CWAT-SSD gains 6.7% (21.0 -> 27.7). On MS-COCO, Our-AT beats the previous-recipe Beni-AT across all detectors (e.g., FCOS +7.5% AP50 under Acls: 17.7 -> 25.2). Detection-specific module choice barely changes robustness once a robust backbone is used, whereas a stronger backbone helps a lot: ConvNeXt-T (48.8% vs ResNet-50's 36.4% AutoAttack accuracy on ImageNet) gives Faster R-CNN a 6.9% AP gain under Acls. Black-box transfer is easier across detection heads than across backbones. Redesigned detectors (more backbone compute, lighter heads) improve both benign and adversarial AP AND raise FPS/lower FLOPs. The insight generalizes: on panoptic segmentation the recipe raises PQ from the prior SOTA 15.9 to 20.6, and their stronger attack drops a standard model's PQ to 1.5 vs the prior weak attack's 12.3.
core_contribution: The paper's central claim is that the adversarial robustness of an object detector is driven primarily by its detection-agnostic backbone, not its detection-specific modules, and that using a backbone ADVERSARIALLY pre-trained on a large upstream classification dataset (e.g., ImageNet) is essential for robust detection. On this basis the authors propose a simple training recipe (Fig. 1c): (1) initialize the detector with an adversarially pre-trained backbone, then (2) adversarially fine-tune the whole detector on the downstream detection dataset using FreeAT (m=4), an AdamW optimizer, and a 0.1x decayed learning rate for the backbone. They also (a) build a unified 'reliable' re-evaluation protocol that shows prior detector-AT defenses (MTD, CWAT, AARD) gave a false sense of security, (b) run a comprehensive investigation across two-stage / one-stage / DETR detectors and different backbones, and (c) redesign detectors that shift compute into the backbone to reach SOTA robustness with higher FPS. NOTE ON PROVENANCE: the paper explicitly distinguishes itself from cited work Salman et al. [31] -- Salman showed robust ImageNet classifiers transfer to better CLEAN accuracy on other CLASSIFICATION datasets; this paper's own novelty is showing robust backbones transfer robustness to downstream DETECTION under attack. The adversarially pre-trained ResNet-50 checkpoint they use as a backbone is itself from Salman et al., i.e., a reused asset, not this paper's contribution.
threat_model: Primary setting is white-box adaptive attack: the adversary knows the full defended detector (architecture, parameters, training data/procedure, intermediate features) and crafts per-pixel perturbations within an L-infinity norm ball ||x - x_adv||_inf <= eps/255. Attacks use PGD with 20 steps and step size alpha = eps/4 (chosen as strongest among eps/10, eps/4, eps/2, eps; the authors argue prior work's alpha=eps was too weak, causing overestimated robustness). Three loss-specific attacks are evaluated: Acls (maximize classification loss only), Areg (maximize regression loss only), and Acwa (class-imbalance-weighted classification+regression, from CWAT). eps=8 on PASCAL VOC, eps=4 on MS-COCO / panoptic segmentation. A secondary black-box transfer threat model is also studied, feeding adversarial examples generated on source detectors into target detectors.
limitations: Purely empirical -- the mechanistic 'error amplification' explanation for why backbones matter is offered only as intuition with a preliminary appendix experiment, not a proof. The recipe reuses off-the-shelf adversarially pre-trained backbones, so its benefit is bounded by (and depends on) the availability/quality of upstream adversarial pre-training, which the authors themselves note is under-explored beyond CIFAR-scale. Robustness is still modest in absolute terms (COCO AP50 under attack ~25-32%) and small objects remain very weak (5-7% AP under Acls). Evaluation, while stronger than prior work, uses PGD variants rather than full AutoAttack (dropped for speed and classifier-specific components), so residual overestimation cannot be fully excluded. Detector-redesign gains are presented as validation of the conclusion rather than an optimized architecture search. Generalization is demonstrated on detection and one panoptic-segmentation model; broader dense-prediction claims are preliminary.

datasets_used: PASCAL VOC (07+12 protocol: 16,551 training images / 20 categories; VOC2007 test = 4,952 images; reported AP50, eps=8)
MS-COCO 2017 (118,287 training images / 80 categories; 5,000 test; COCO-style AP + AP50/AP75/APS/APM/APL, eps=4)
ImageNet (upstream classification dataset for adversarial pre-training of backbones)
quantitative_results: PASCAL VOC Faster R-CNN AP50 under Acls: STD 0.1, MTD 18.2, CWAT 19.1, VANAT 19.0; with Our Recipe MTD 30.8 / CWAT 32.2 / VANAT 32.2
AARD claimed 41.5% AP50 under Acls in its paper but scored 0.7 AP50 (worse than STD's 1.3) on SSD under the stronger PGD alpha=eps/4 attack
SSD CWAT AP50 under Acls improved 6.7 points with the recipe: 21.0 -> 27.7
Ablation (Table II, Faster R-CNN): upstream-adversarial pre-training + AdamW + 0.1x backbone LR = 69.7 benign / 32.2 Acls AP50, vs upstream-benign 1x SGD 44.6 / 15.7
MS-COCO FCOS AP50 under Acls: Beni-AT 17.7 vs Our-AT 25.2 (+7.5); DN-DETR 17.5 -> 27.4; Faster R-CNN 22.1 -> 29.3
ConvNeXt-T vs ResNet-50 upstream AutoAttack accuracy on ImageNet: 48.8% vs 36.4%; yields Faster R-CNN +6.9% AP under Acls on COCO
Redesigned Faster R-CNN* vs Faster R-CNN (COCO): benign AP 35.1 vs 34.3, Acwa AP50 37.1 vs 35.9, with FPS 25.6 vs 25.4 and lower total FLOPs (169.7G vs 209.1G)
Panoptic segmentation: Our-AT PanopticFPN PQ 20.6 vs prior SOTA Daza et al. 15.9; the paper's Acls attack cut a standard model's PQ to 1.5 vs Daza et al.'s attack 12.3
baselines_compared: VANAT (vanilla detection-loss adversarial training, authors' baseline)
Beni-AT (benignly pre-trained backbone AT = previous-works recipe) vs Our-AT
Full PGD-AT (20x training cost) as an upper-bound comparison to FreeAT recipe
Daza et al. (prior SOTA adversarially trained panoptic segmentation)
